paragraphs.text: This is a LibriVox recording. All LibriVox recordings are in the public domain. For more information or to volunteer, please visit librivox.org.
This recording is by Mark Smith of Simpsonville, South Carolina. Uncle Remus by Joel Chandler Harris. Chapter 2. The Wonderful Tar-Baby Story. Didn't the fox never catch the rabbit, Uncle Remus?

Asked the little boy, the next evening. He come mighty nigh it, honey, sho's you born, Brer Fox did. One day, arter Brer Rabbit fooled him wid dat calamus root, Brer Fox went to work en Got him some tar, and mix it with some turpentine. He fix up a contraption what he call a tar-baby; and he took dish-yer tar-baby, and he sot her in de big road, en den he lay off in de bushes fur to see what de news was gwyne to be. En he didn' hatter wait long, neder; 'kase by en by, here comes Brer Rabbit basin down de road.

Lippity-clipity, clipity-lippity— just as sassy as a jaybird. Brer Fox he lay low. Brer Rabbit come prancing along till he spied de tar-baby, en den he fotch up on his behind legs like he was stonished. De tar-baby she sot dar, she did; en Brer Fox he lay low. Mornin', says Brer Rabbit, says he.
Nice weder dis mornin', says he. Tar-baby she ain't sayin' noth'n. Brer Fox he lay low. How does yo' symptoms seem to segershwate? Says Brer Rabbit, says he.
Brer Fox he wink his eyes slow, and lay low; and Tar-baby She ain't sayin' noth'n. How you come on, den? Is you deaf? Says Brer Rabbit, says he. 'Cause if you is I can holler louder, says he.

Tar-baby stay still, and Brer Fox he lay low. You're stuck-up, dat's what you is, says Brer Rabbit, says he; en I'm goin' ter k'yer you, dat's what I'm a-goin' ter do, says he. Brer Fox he sort of chuckled in his stomach, he did; but tar-baby ain't saying nothing. I'm going to learn you to talk to 'spectable folks if it's de last act, says Brer Rabbit, says he. If you don't take off dat hat, and tell me howdy, I'm going to bust you wide open, says he.

Tar-baby stays still. And Brer Fox he lay low. Brer Rabbit kep' on axin' him, and de tar-baby she kep' on sayin' noth'n. 'Twixt presently Brer Rabbit drar' back wid his fist, he did, en blip! He tuck her side o' de head.

Right dah's whar he broke his molasses jug. His fist stuck, en he can't pull loose. De tar hilt him; but Tar-baby she stay still, en Brer Fox he lay low. If'n you doan' lemme loose, I'll knock you agin, says Brer Rabbit, says he; en wid dat he fotcher a wipe wid de udder han, en dat one stuk. Tar-baby she still ain't say noth'n, en Brer Fox he lay low.

Turn me loose 'fo' I kick de natural stuffin' out o' you! Says Brer Rabbit, says he. But de tar-baby she ain't sayin' noth'n. She just hild on, en de Brer Rabbit lose de use un his feet in de same way. Brer Fox he lay low.

Den Brer Rabbit squall out dat if de tar-baby don' turn him loose he'd But her crank-sided, and then he butted, and his head got stuck. Then Brer Fox he sauntered forth, looking just as innocent as one of your mammy's mockingbirds. Howdy, Brer Rabbit, says Brer Fox, says he. You look sort of stuck up this morning, says he, and then he rolled on the ground and laughed and laughed till he couldn't laugh no more. I 'spect you'll take dinner wid me dis time, Brer Rabbit.

I done laid in some calamus-root, en I ain' gwyneer take no 'scuse, says Brer Fox, says he. Here Uncle Remus paused, and drew a 2-pound yam out of the ashes. Did the fox eat the rabbit? Asked the little boy to whom the story had been told. Oh, dat's all de fur de tale goes, replied the old man.
He might, en den he mightn't. Some say Judge Barr come 'long en loosed 'em; some say he didn't. I hear Miss Sally callin'; you better run 'long.
